Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Data availability states the UGT genes identified in this study were deposited under GenBank accession numbers MT551198 to MT551202; PnUGT1 is one of the five validated genes, so MT551198 is supported by the listed range/order.

Source_Species.reason: The paper studies and clones UGT genes from Panax notoginseng.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source species throughout the paper is Panax notoginseng.

Applied_Species.reason: The experimental and omics work is on Panax notoginseng.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species in this study is Panax notoginseng.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: PnUGT1 is explicitly placed in ginsenoside biosynthesis; the paper also discusses candidate genes for notoginsenoside biosynthesis more broadly, so 'ginsenoside/notoginsenoside biosynthesis' is acceptable.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: PnUGT1 is a UGT functioning in the glycosylation/post-modification part of triterpene saponin biosynthesis.

Enzyme_Name.reason: PnUGT1 is one of the cloned UGT genes; UGT denotes UDP-glycosyltransferase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper refers to UGT genes and later to genes containing the conserved GT domain; Family 1 UGT/GT domain is consistent with the paper.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided for PnUGT1 or the validated UGTs in the paper.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results explicitly state PnUGT1 adds a glucosyl group at C20 to PPD and PPT to produce CK and F1, and catalyzes Rh2 to F2.

Primary_Substrate.reason: PPD, PPT, and Rh2 are explicitly named as substrates for PnUGT1; UDP-glucose is part of the UGT assay mixture in Methods.

Primary_Product.reason: CK, F1, and F2 are explicitly reported as products of PnUGT1 reactions.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: These products are ginsenosides: CK, F1, and F2.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The paper states most saponin compounds in P. notoginseng are triterpene glycosides; these ginsenosides belong to that class.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper says P. notoginseng saponins have extensive/good pharmacological effects, but it does not assign medicinal functions specifically to CK, F1, and F2 in the Results.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper reports most pathway genes are highly expressed in flowers, and some UGTs/root tissues are implicated, but it does not directly show accumulation sites for CK/F1/F2 or for PnUGT1 specifically.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: PnUGT1 directly forms the reported ginsenoside products in enzymatic assays.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: UGT-mediated glycosylation is described as the ultimate/downstream post-modification step in saponin biosynthesis.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Discussion explicitly says the ultimate step is glycosylation catalyzed by UGTs and that this is the most critical step determining structure and pharmacological effects.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: PnUGT1 is shown to produce specific ginsenosides by C20 glycosylation and further glycosylation to F2.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Methods and Results support cloning, E. coli expression, crude enzyme assays with UDP-glucose and substrates, and UPLC/Q-TOF-MS identification.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three statements are explicitly supported: CK/F1 formation, Rh2 to F2 as first reported new function in P. notoginseng, and inclusion among five experimentally validated UGTs.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper does not specifically place PnUGT1 in an expression category beyond noting it is in the green module; detailed tissue-high expression for PnUGT1 itself is not given.

Omics_Data.reason: Genome data accessions PRJNA658419 and GWHAOSA00000000 are explicitly provided; transcriptome RNA-seq is part of the study but no accession is stated.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Transcriptome analysis used 1- to 4-year-old plants and listed tissues including root, stem, leaf, flower, rhizome, fibril, periderm, phloem, and tubercle.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results state PnUGT1 functions are consistent with UGTPg100 and UGTPg101 from P. ginseng, and the last four genes have also been shown catalytic in S. cerevisiae in cited work.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a plausible application, but the paper does not explicitly discuss metabolic engineering/breeding use of PnUGT1 for CK/F1/F2 production.

corrections.reason: No EC number is provided for PnUGT1 or the validated UGTs in the paper.
